title: Sarah Cahill (pianist)

infobox.value: [San Francisco Conservatory](/wiki/San_Francisco_Conservatory "San Francisco Conservatory") [University of Michigan](/wiki/University_of_Michigan "University of Michigan")

(born 1960) is an American pianist based in the [Bay Area](/wiki/San_Francisco_Bay_Area "San Francisco Bay Area").[[1]](#cite_note-1) She has also worked as a writer on music and as a radio show host.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

American pianist based in the Bay Area

| Sarah Cahill | |
| --- | --- |
| Sarah Cahill performing in December 2021 | |
| Born | 1960 (age 65–66)  [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.") |
| Alma mater | [San Francisco Conservatory](/wiki/San_Francisco_Conservatory "San Francisco Conservatory") [University of Michigan](/wiki/University_of_Michigan "University of Michigan") |
| Occupations | Pianist, radio personality |
| Spouse | [John Sanborn](/wiki/John_Sanborn_(media_artist) "John Sanborn (media artist)") |
| Relatives | [James Cahill](/wiki/James_Cahill_(art_historian) "James Cahill (art historian)") (father) |

**Sarah Cahill** (born 1960) is an American pianist based in the [Bay Area](/wiki/San_Francisco_Bay_Area "San Francisco Bay Area").[[1]](#cite_note-1) She has also worked as a writer on music and as a radio show host.

## Early life and education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sarah_Cahill_(pianist)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life and education")]

Born in [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C."), Sarah Cahill moved to [Berkeley, California](/wiki/Berkeley,_California "Berkeley, California") when her father [James Cahill](/wiki/James_Cahill_(art_historian) "James Cahill (art historian)") became Professor of Chinese Art History at the [University of California, Berkeley](/wiki/University_of_California,_Berkeley "University of California, Berkeley").[[2]](#cite_note-tribute-2)[[3]](#cite_note-NYT_Tireless-3) She was drawn to music as her father owned an extensive collection of records, including rare recordings of composers and pianists such as [Stravinsky](/wiki/Stravinsky "Stravinsky"), [Prokofiev](/wiki/Prokofiev "Prokofiev"), [Bartók](/wiki/Bart%C3%B3k "Bartók"), [Artur Schnabel](/wiki/Artur_Schnabel "Artur Schnabel"), [Walter Gieseking](/wiki/Walter_Gieseking "Walter Gieseking") and [Clara Haskil](/wiki/Clara_Haskil "Clara Haskil"). Cahill began her formal piano studies at the age of six, and at seven she began studying with Sharon Mann. By twelve, she had started performing concertos with several local orchestras. At sixteen, she played [Bach](/wiki/Bach "Bach")’s D major Toccata at the Sommermusikwochen chamber music festival in [Trogen, Switzerland](/wiki/Trogen,_Switzerland "Trogen, Switzerland").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Cahill skipped her final year of high school to join the [San Francisco Conservatory](/wiki/San_Francisco_Conservatory_of_Music "San Francisco Conservatory of Music"), where [John Adams](/wiki/John_Adams_(composer) "John Adams (composer)") composed *[China Gates](/wiki/China_Gates "China Gates")* for her. She finished her academic studies at the [University of Michigan](/wiki/University_of_Michigan "University of Michigan"), where she continued her musical training with [Theodore Lettvin](/wiki/Theodore_Lettvin "Theodore Lettvin").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Music career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sarah_Cahill_(pianist)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Music career")]

Sarah Cahill has commissioned, premiered and recorded numerous works for solo piano.[[3]](#cite_note-NYT_Tireless-3) Compositions dedicated to her include [John Adams](/wiki/John_Coolidge_Adams "John Coolidge Adams")’ *[China Gates](/wiki/China_Gates "China Gates")*, [Frederic Rzewski](/wiki/Frederic_Rzewski "Frederic Rzewski")’s *Snippets 2*, [Pauline Oliveros](/wiki/Pauline_Oliveros "Pauline Oliveros")’ *Quintuplets Play Pen*, and [Kyle Gann](/wiki/Kyle_Gann "Kyle Gann")'s *Private Dances and On Reading Emerson*. She has also premiered works by [Lou Harrison](/wiki/Lou_Harrison "Lou Harrison"), [Terry Riley](/wiki/Terry_Riley "Terry Riley"), [Evan Ziporyn](/wiki/Evan_Ziporyn "Evan Ziporyn"), [Julia Wolfe](/wiki/Julia_Wolfe "Julia Wolfe"), [Ingram Marshall](/wiki/Ingram_Marshall "Ingram Marshall"), [Ursula Mamlok](/wiki/Ursula_Mamlok "Ursula Mamlok"), [George Lewis](/wiki/George_Lewis_(trombonist) "George Lewis (trombonist)"), [Leo Ornstein](/wiki/Leo_Ornstein "Leo Ornstein") and many others.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In late 2008 and 2009 Cahill developed and performed a new project known under two titles, A Sweeter Music, and Notes on the War: The Piano Protests, where she asked composers for piano music on the subject of peace. The second title was printed in *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")*, but was not Cahill's original title.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-sweet_facts-5) Commissioned composers include Preben Antonsen, Michael Byron, [Paul Dresher](/wiki/Paul_Dresher "Paul Dresher"), [Ingram Marshall](/wiki/Ingram_Marshall "Ingram Marshall"), [Jerome Kitzke](/wiki/Jerome_Kitzke "Jerome Kitzke"), [Mamoru Fujieda](/wiki/Mamoru_Fujieda "Mamoru Fujieda"), [Kyle Gann](/wiki/Kyle_Gann "Kyle Gann"), [Peter Garland](/wiki/Peter_Garland_(composer) "Peter Garland (composer)"), [Phil Kline](/wiki/Phil_Kline "Phil Kline"), [Jerome Kitzke](/wiki/Jerome_Kitzke "Jerome Kitzke"), [Meredith Monk](/wiki/Meredith_Monk "Meredith Monk"), [Pauline Oliveros](/wiki/Pauline_Oliveros "Pauline Oliveros"), [Yoko Ono](/wiki/Yoko_Ono "Yoko Ono"), [Larry Polansky](/wiki/Larry_Polansky "Larry Polansky"), [Bernice Johnson Reagon](/wiki/Bernice_Johnson_Reagon "Bernice Johnson Reagon"), [The Residents](/wiki/The_Residents "The Residents"), [Terry Riley](/wiki/Terry_Riley "Terry Riley"), [Frederic Rzewski](/wiki/Frederic_Rzewski "Frederic Rzewski") and [Carl Stone](/wiki/Carl_Stone "Carl Stone"). In later performances of A Sweeter Music, Cahill's spouse [John Sanborn](/wiki/John_Sanborn_(media_artist) "John Sanborn (media artist)") contributed video content to accompany the music, displayed across three screens and synchronized music.[[5]](#cite_note-sweet_facts-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Other projects developed by Cahill include Playdate, Bay Area Pianists and Garden of Memory. Playdate is a group of commissioned pieces about childhood combined with classical works; the commission of an evening of new scores for four hands by [Terry Riley](/wiki/Terry_Riley "Terry Riley"), performed with pianist Joseph Kubera; and a concert of recent [Italian](/wiki/Italian_culture "Italian culture") music, featuring premieres by [Luciano Chessa](/wiki/Luciano_Chessa "Luciano Chessa"), Andrea Morricone, and others.[[7]](#cite_note-7) She founded the Bay Area Pianists in 1993. In 1996, in association with New Music Bay Area*,* Cahill created the annual Garden of Memory walk-through concert at the [Julia Morgan](/wiki/Julia_Morgan "Julia Morgan")-designed [Chapel of the Chimes](/wiki/Chapel_of_the_Chimes_(Oakland,_California) "Chapel of the Chimes (Oakland, California)") wherein audience members move through the environment with new music ensembles performing simultaneously throughout the spaces.[[8]](#cite_note-8) In 2003 she co-curated the Berkeley Edge Fest at [Cal Performances](/wiki/Cal_Performances "Cal Performances").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Cahill investigated the impact early 20th century American modernists had on the composers of her time and explored these influences in concert programs at the Miller Theater at [Columbia University](/wiki/Columbia_University "Columbia University"), [Lincoln Center](/wiki/Lincoln_Center "Lincoln Center"), [Merkin Hall](/wiki/Merkin_Hall "Merkin Hall"), Galapagos Art Space in New York City, [Spoleto Festival USA](/wiki/Spoleto_Festival_USA "Spoleto Festival USA"), the [Phillips Collection](/wiki/Phillips_Collection "Phillips Collection"), the [Freer Gallery](/wiki/Freer_Gallery "Freer Gallery") (part of the Smithsonian Piano 300 gala), and at the [Other Minds](/wiki/Other_Minds_(organization) "Other Minds (organization)") Festival in San Francisco. She has also performed at the Nuovi Spazi Musicali Festival at the [American Academy in Rome](/wiki/American_Academy_in_Rome "American Academy in Rome"), the [Santa Fe Chamber Music Festival](/wiki/Santa_Fe_Chamber_Music_Festival "Santa Fe Chamber Music Festival"), and at the Pacific Crossings Festival in [Tokyo, Japan](/wiki/Tokyo,_Japan "Tokyo, Japan").[[9]](#cite_note-MIT_bio-9)

In December 2021, Cahill performed a seven-hour marathon performance *The Future is Female* comprising over seventy pieces by female composers at [BAMPFA](/wiki/BAMPFA "BAMPFA"), including works she commissioned by [Regina Harris Baiocchi](/wiki/Regina_Harris_Baiocchi "Regina Harris Baiocchi"), [Mary Watkins](/wiki/Mary_Watkins "Mary Watkins"), and [Theresa Wong](/wiki/Theresa_Wong "Theresa Wong").[[10]](#cite_note-10)

## Other work

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sarah_Cahill_(pianist)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Other work")]

As a radio personality, Cahill has hosted weekly radio shows on the classical and contemporary music scenes on both [KPFA](/wiki/KPFA "KPFA") in [Berkeley](/wiki/Berkeley,_California "Berkeley, California"), where her program was cited as "One of the 100 Best Things in the Bay Area" by [Citysearch](/wiki/Citysearch "Citysearch"), and on [KALW](/wiki/KALW "KALW") in [San Francisco](/wiki/San_Francisco "San Francisco").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Cahill has written music reviews for Gramophone Explorations, Historical Performance, ClassicsToday.com, *[The New Grove Dictionary of Music and Musicians](/wiki/The_New_Grove_Dictionary_of_Music_and_Musicians "The New Grove Dictionary of Music and Musicians")* and other international publications, in addition to liner notes for recordings by [John Adams](/wiki/John_Adams_(composer) "John Adams (composer)"), [Terry Riley](/wiki/Terry_Riley "Terry Riley"), and others. In 1985 she became the music critic for the *[East Bay Express](/wiki/East_Bay_Express "East Bay Express")* and has been published in the *[San Francisco Chronicle](/wiki/San_Francisco_Chronicle "San Francisco Chronicle")*, *[Village Voice](/wiki/Village_Voice "Village Voice")* Literary Supplement, and others.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sarah_Cahill_(pianist)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Cahill is married to the media artist [John Sanborn](/wiki/John_Sanborn_(media_artist) "John Sanborn (media artist)").[[11]](#cite_note-11)

## Discography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sarah_Cahill_(pianist)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Discography")]

* *[Miroirs](/wiki/Miroirs "Miroirs")* and *[Gaspard de la Nuit](/wiki/Gaspard_de_la_Nuit "Gaspard de la Nuit")* by [Maurice Ravel](/wiki/Maurice_Ravel "Maurice Ravel") (New Albion, 1997)
* *[Ruth Crawford](/wiki/Ruth_Crawford_Seeger "Ruth Crawford Seeger"): 9 Preludes; [Johanna Beyer](/wiki/Johanna_Beyer "Johanna Beyer"): Dissonant Counterpoint, Gebrauchs-Musik* (New Albion, 2001)
* *New Music: Piano Compositions* by [Henry Cowell](/wiki/Henry_Cowell "Henry Cowell") (New Albion, 2003)
* *Long Night* by [Kyle Gann](/wiki/Kyle_Gann "Kyle Gann") (Cold Blue, 2005)
* *[Ingram Marshall](/wiki/Ingram_Marshall "Ingram Marshall"): Savage Altars* (New Albion, 2006)
* *[Kyle Gann](/wiki/Kyle_Gann "Kyle Gann"): Private Dances* (New Albion, 2008)
* *[Marc Blitzstein](/wiki/Marc_Blitzstein "Marc Blitzstein"): First Life – Rare Early Works* ([Other Minds](/wiki/Other_Minds_(organization) "Other Minds (organization)"), 2009)
* *A Sweeter Music* ([Other Minds](/wiki/Other_Minds_(organization) "Other Minds (organization)"), 2013)
* *[Mamoru Fujieda](/wiki/Mamoru_Fujieda "Mamoru Fujieda"): Patterns of Plants* (Pinna, 2014)
* *Eighty Trips Around the Sun: Music by and for [Terry Riley](/wiki/Terry_Riley "Terry Riley")* (Irritable Hedgehog, 2017)
* *The Future Is Female, Vol.1 In Nature* (First Hand, 2022)
* *The Future Is Female, Vol.2 The Dance* (First Hand, 2022)
* *The Future Is Female, Vol.3 At Play* (First Hand, 2023)
